result: I keep thinking about the way the sky looks at 5:47 a.m. in late April, when the night is almost gone but hasn’t quite conceded. There’s a bruised stripe of indigo above the eastern roofline, and the first blackbird is tuning his throat like he’s afraid of waking anyone. I stand at the kitchen window in socked feet, coffee hissing behind me, and feel time dilate the way it did when I was seven and feverish: the universe breathing in, the universe breathing out, everything hinged on a single heartbeat. I’m neither child nor adult then, just a witness suspended in the hyphen. The day hasn’t pinned its agenda to my chest yet; no emails, no headlines, no polite ghosts of unfinished conversations. Only the smell of warm cardboard from the bakery below, the soft click of the thermostat, the neighbor’s cat plotting murder beneath the lilac. I could step outside barefoot, leave footprints in the dew, and never come back to the version of me who went to bed worrying about interest rates. Instead, I stay, because the coffee is ready and the mug my grandmother gave me is still intact, and because wonder is a muscle that atrophies if you don’t flex it against the ordinary. So I watch the indigo fade to pearl, and I promise the day—quietly, ridiculously—that I will meet it halfway, that I will not let the news cycle amputate my capacity for small, fierce gladness. The blackbird finishes his scale. The kettle clicks off. The hyphen holds.
